title: Recent Progress On Mean Curvature Flow
speaker: Bruce Kleiner
abstract: An evolving surface is a mean curvature flow if the normal component of its velocity field is given by the mean curvature. First introduced in the physics literature in the 1950s, the mean curvature flow equation has been studied intensely by mathematicians since the 1970s with the aim of understanding singularity formation and developing a rigorous mathematical treatment of flow through singularities. I will discuss progress in the last few years which has led to the solution of several longstanding conjectures, including the Multiplicity One Conjecture. This is based on joint work with Richard Bamler.

proof_stages.label: Classical mean-curvature-flow framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines mean curvature flow as velocity equal to mean curvature vector, gives equivalent graph, parametrized, and gradient-flow formulations, and records short-time existence, curvature blow-up, avoidance, and finite extinction for compact flows.

proof_stages.label: Singularity models from rescaling
proof_stages.summary: Elementary singular scenarios and parabolic scaling lead to the notion of self-shrinking solitons as the candidate local models for finite-time singularities.

proof_stages.label: Huisken monotonicity and tangent-flow compactness
proof_stages.summary: Huisken's Gaussian-area monotonicity defines densities and implies that blow-up limits with constant Gaussian area are shrinkers, while Ilmanen's compactness theorem gives subsequential tangent flows as integer-multiplicity shrinking solitons, isolating multiplicity one as the key obstruction to smooth blow-up analysis.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural program for flowing through singularities
proof_stages.summary: The neck-pinch picture motivates continuing mean curvature flow past singularities and leads to the conjectural package of existence with small singular set, multiplicity one, generic spherical/cylindrical singularities, and simple behavior for initial two-spheres.

proof_stages.label: Weak-flow frameworks and earlier regularity theory
proof_stages.summary: Brakke flows and level-set flows are introduced as weak formulations with useful existence, monotonicity, compactness, and avoidance properties but pathologies such as mass drop and fattening, while White's mean-convex theory and later refinements establish the desired conclusions in important special cases.

proof_stages.label: Almost regular flows and key theorems
proof_stages.summary: The new almost regular flow notion is defined by weak-set-flow behavior plus spacetime L2 control of the inverse parabolic regularity scale, and the main new theorems give compactness and multiplicity-one tangent and limit flows.

proof_stages.label: Consequences resolving the conjectures
proof_stages.summary: Combining the almost regular flow theorems with recent work yields existence for outer/inner and nonfattening level-set flows, Hausdorff-dimension-one singular sets, generic round-sphere/round-cylinder tangent flows, and uniqueness plus simple singularities in the S2 case.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicity-one proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch explains how higher multiplicity is ruled out by analyzing possible sheet-separation and catenoidal-neck scenarios, using log-separation supersolutions, Gaussian subsolutions, blended integral estimates, and a spacetime decomposition that discards bad regions.
